comments.text: For $\binom{n}{k}$ with defined deficiency (i.e. $\gcd\left(\binom{n}{k},\,k!\right)=1$), we shall write $\delta(n,k)$ for its associated deficiency. This is probably useless, but I can give a resolution to the second problem under two rather strong conjectures: $\textbf{Conjecture A}$ (Strengthening of the $xyz_{\mathrm{fin}}$-conjecture of Lagarias-Soundararajan). There exists $\kappa_0>1$ such that for all $\varepsilon>0$ there are only finitely many primitive integer triples $X+Y=Z$ with $$\max\{p\in\mathbb{P}:p\mid XYZ\}=:S(X,Y,Z)<(\log H(X,Y,Z))^{\kappa_0-\varepsilon},$$ where $H(X,Y,Z):=\max\{|X|,\,|Y|,\,|Z|\}$. $\textbf{Conjecture B}$ (Height forcing). Let $h_2(k)$ be the least $n\geq 2k$ such that $\binom{n}{k}$ has defined deficiency $\delta(n,k)\geq 2$. There exists $\alpha>1/\kappa_0$ such that for all sufficiently large $k$, $$h_2(k)<\infty\implies\log h_2(k)\geq ck^{\alpha}$$for some fixed $c>0$. (By PNT, we can at least show that if there is such an $\alpha>0$, then $\alpha\leq 1$). Now, we state our result: $\textbf{Theorem}$. Assume Conjecture A and Conjecture B. Then, there are only finitely many pairs $(n,k)$ with $n\geq 2k$ and $\delta(n,k)\geq 2$. $\textit{Proof.}$ Suppose, for contradiction, that there are infinitely many such $(n,k)$. Fix one with $\delta(n,k)\geq 2$, then $\exists i,j\in\mathbb{N}$ with $i\neq j$ such that $x:= n-i$ and $y:=n-j$ are both $k$-smooth, lying in $(n-k,n]$. Set $\Delta:=\left|x-y\right|=\left|i-j\right|$, so $1\leq\Delta<k$. Since $\Delta<k$, all prime factors of $\Delta$ are $\leq k$, hence $\Delta$ is $k$-smooth. Now, consider the equation $y+\Delta=x$. Let $d:=\gcd(y,\Delta)$, and set $$A:=\frac{y}{d},\qquad B:=\frac{\Delta}{d},\qquad C:=\frac{x}{d},$$then $A+B=C$ is primitive. Because $x,y,\Delta$ are $k$-smooth and dividing by $d$ removes only primes $\leq k$, we have $S(A,B,C)\leq k$. Moreover, since $x\in(n-k,n]$, and $d\leq\Delta<k$, $$H(A,B,C)\geq C=\frac{x}{d}\geq\frac{n-k}{k}.$$Thus, $\log H\geq\log n-\mathcal{O}(\log k)$. Now, invoke Conjecture B: Since $\delta(n,k)\geq 2$, we have $\log n\gg k^{\alpha}$, hence $\log H\gg k^{\alpha}\iff k\ll(\log H)^{1/\alpha}$. Choose $\varepsilon>0$ s.t. $\alpha(\kappa_0-\varepsilon)>1$ (which is possible as we have assumed $\alpha>1/\kappa_0$). Then, for all sufficiently large members of the infinite family, $$S(A,B,C)\leq k\ll(\log H)^{1/\alpha}<(\log H)^{\kappa_0-\varepsilon}.$$This produces infinitely many primitive triples violating Conjecture A, a contradiction. $\square$

FWIW, I think, as in [ELS88], that we want $n\geq 2k$, otherwise the second question is trivial: For a prime $p$, $\binom{p}{p-1}=p$ has deficiency $p-2$, so there are infinitely many with deficiency $>1$. Also, the current listing of $\binom{8113}{28},\binom{8114}{28},\binom{96022}{42}$ as having deficiency $2$ seems incorrect to me. They appear to have undefined deficiency: $\binom{8113}{28}$ and $\binom{8114}{28}$ are divisible by $2,3,11,13,17,19$, and $\binom{96022}{42}$ is divisble by $2,3,5,7,11,13,17,29,41$. I believe the intended entries are $\binom{8413}{28}$, $\binom{8414}{28}$, and $\binom{96622}{42}$, which all appear to have deficiency $2$. Moreover, $\binom{174}{12}$ and $\binom{239}{14}$ also have deficiency $2$. For $n\leq 10^5$, there are $58$ deficiency $1$ examples, $8$ deficiency $2$ examples, $6$ deficiency $3$ examples, and $1$ example for deficiences $4$ and $9$ each. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
